# Cybercrime & Botnets

> *Modern attacks are a business. Understanding the economics — who profits, and how — tells you where the defences have to go.*

## What this chapter covers

By the end you should be able to:

- Explain what a **botnet** is, how one is built and monetised (spam, DDoS, pay-per-install).
- Describe the **cybercrime economy** — the division of labour behind modern attacks.
- Explain, at a high level, a real botnet case (e.g. **Mirai**, Torpig).
- Connect forensic traces (email headers, document metadata) to attributing an attack.

## Outline

*(Draft outline. **Refresh flag — highest
priority:** the malware-volume statistics are dated 2015/2017/2021 and age every year. Replace
with a current figure or reframe as "growth over time" so the chapter doesn't rot.)*

1. Cybercrime as an economy — motives, markets, division of labour.
2. Botnets — command-and-control; building and renting them; spam and DDoS.
3. Monetisation — pay-per-install, ransomware, credential theft.
4. Case studies — a modern IoT botnet (Mirai) and a classic (Torpig/Zeus).
5. Forensics and attribution — what traces attacks leave behind.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Play it

*Incident Zero*'s **Forensics** module has you chase attribution and rebuild a timeline while
protecting the chain of custody — mishandle the evidence trail and you lose the case, even
when you know who did it. → [incidentzero.retroverse.studio](https://incidentzero.retroverse.studio/)
:::

## Where this connects

- **software security** — malware and trojans; **network security** — the network traffic botnets generate.

## Questions to consider

1. Why is a **botnet** so much more valuable to a criminal than a single compromised machine?
2. Modern cybercrime has a "division of labour" — different people write malware, rent
   servers, and cash out. Why does that specialisation make attacks harder to stop?
3. What kind of **trace** does an attacker most often forget to hide? (You'll test this in the
   lab.)
